title: Pause Is Not Pivot
thesis: The Fed held rates steady, but the policy message remained restrictive because inflation is still above target.
viewerQuestion: Why did a hold not equal relief?

hook.headline: The Fed paused. That was not a pivot.

metrics.label: Gap to Fed target
metrics.sourceName: Derived from BEA and Federal Reserve target

scenes.headline: The Fed paused. That was not a pivot.
scenes.caption: The hold was not the relief trade.

scenes.headline: The decision was steady. The message was not soft.
scenes.caption: Markets wanted relief. The Fed gave conditions.

scenes.headline: Inflation is still above the line that matters.
scenes.caption: PCE inflation: 4.1% vs 2.0% target.

scenes.headline: That keeps rate-sensitive trades exposed.
scenes.caption: Growth multiples still have to respect rate math.

narration: The Fed paused. That was not a pivot. Rates stayed at 3.50 to 3.75 percent, but the decision was not the whole message. Markets wanted relief. The Fed gave conditions. Inflation is still above the line that matters: PCE inflation at 4.1 percent versus a 2 percent target. That gap keeps rate-sensitive trades exposed. The signal is not, cuts are here. The signal is, inflation has to prove it. Not investment advice.

sources.name: Federal Reserve
sources.usedFor: June FOMC target range and statement tone

compliance.sourceSummary: Sources: Federal Reserve FOMC statement, Jun 17; BEA Personal Income and Outlays, May 2026. Verify final figures before publishing.

platformCaption: The Fed held rates, but the message was not easy money. The key visual: PCE inflation at 4.1% versus the Fed's 2% target. A pause is not automatically a pivot. Not investment advice. Sources: Federal Reserve; BEA.
